reasoning: I will give a concise definition of pancreatic enzymes, list the main examples (amylase, lipase, proteases), note their zymogen activation in the intestine, and mention their role and a brief clinical note.
output:
Pancreatic enzymes are digestive enzymes produced by the exocrine pancreas and released into the small intestine to help digest food. The major pancreatic enzymes are:
- Amylase (pancreatic amylase): digests carbohydrates (starches) into simple sugars.
- Lipase (pancreatic lipase): digests fats into fatty acids and monoglycerides.
- Proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin, carboxypeptidases): digest proteins into peptides and amino acids.

These enzymes are secreted as inactive precursors (zymogens; e.g., trypsinogen, chymotrypsinogen) and activated in the small intestine (trypsinogen is activated to trypsin, which then activates the others). They are part of pancreatic juice produced by the pancreas and work best in an alkaline environment provided by bicarbonate in the juice. Deficiency or malfunction of these enzymes can lead to malabsorption and fatty stools.
